en.extract:
The buff-cheeked greenlet (Pachysylvia muscicapina) is a species of bird in the family Vireonidae, the vireos, greenlets, and shrike-babblers. It is found in Bolivia, Brazil, French Guiana, Guyana, Suriname, and Venezuela.


Taxonomy and systematics
The buff-cheeked greenlet was originally described in 1873 as Hylophilus muscicapinus.
The buff-cheeked greenlet has two subspecies, the nominate P. m. muscicapina (Sclater, PL & Salvin, 1873) and P. m. griseifrons (Snethlage, E, 1907).


Description
The buff-cheeked greenlet is 11.5 to 12 cm (4.5 to 4.7 in) long and weighs 9.5 to 13.5 g (0.34 to 0.48 oz). The sexes have the same plumage. Adults of the nominate subspecies have a gray-brown forehead and a blue-gray crown and nape. They have a buffy supercilium and blackish gray mottled ear coverts on an otherwise buffy-brown face. Their upperparts are greenish that is lighter on the rump. Their wings' primaries and secondaries are dark blackish gray with wide greenish edges on the outer webs. Their tail is dull greenish. Their chin and throat are off-white, their breast grayish buff, their belly grayish white, and their flanks yellowish. Subspecies P. m. griseifrons has a pure gray forehead and a deeper buffy rufous supercilium and face than the nominate with brighter green upperparts and a more buffy throat and breast. Both subspecies have a sepia, brown, or gray iris, a blackish maxilla, a pinkish mandible, and pale pinkish or light gray legs and feet.


Distribution and habitat
The nominate subspecies of the buff-cheeked greenlet is the more northerly of the two. It is found in eastern Venezuela's Amazonas, Bolívar, and southern Delta Amacuro states east to the Atlantic through the Guianas and northern Brazil north of the Amazon. Subspecies P. m. griseifrons is found in Brazil south of the Amazon between the Madeira and Tapajós rivers and south to Mato Grosso and southern Goiás and into extreme northeastern Bolivia. The species inhabits humid terra firme and várzea forest where it frequents the mid-story to canopy. In Brazil it ranges in elevation from sea level to 600 m (2,000 ft). It reaches 1,100 m (3,600 ft) in Venezuela.


Behavior


Movement
The buff-cheeked greenlet is believed to be a sedentary year-round resident.


Feeding
The buff-cheeked greenlet's diet has not been fully examined but is known to include insects and spiders. It feeds actively, often hanging upside-down to glean prey from leaves. It usually forages in pairs or small family groups and frequently joins mixed-species feeding flocks.


Breeding
The buff-cheeked greenlet is believed to breed between December and May in Venezuela. Nothing else is known about the species' breeding biology.


Vocalization
The buff-cheeked greenlet's song is described as a "high, warbling weet-oh-weeréet".


Status
The IUCN has assessed the buff-cheeked greenlet as being of Least Concern. It has a large range; its population size is not known and is believed to be decreasing. No immediate threats have been identified. It is considered "frequent to uncommon" in Brazil and "common" in Venezuela. 


References

The '''buff-cheeked greenlet''' ('''''Pachysylvia muscicapina''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Vireonidae]], the vireos, greenlets, and shrike-babblers. It is found in [[Bolivia]], [[Brazil]], [[French Guiana]], [[Guyana]], [[Suriname]], and [[Venezuela]].<ref name=IOC15.1>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/shrikes/ |title=Vireos, shrike-babblers |website=IOC World Bird List |version =v 15.1 | editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen |date=March 2025 |access-date=3 March 2025 }}</ref>

==Taxonomy and systematics==

The buff-cheeked greenlet was [[Species description|originally described]] in 1873 as ''Hylophilus muscicapinus''.<ref>{{cite book | last=Sclater |first=Philip Lutley |last2=Salvin |first2=Osbert | date= 1873 | title=Nomenclator avium neotropicalium: sive avium quae in regione neotropica hucusque repertae sunt nomina systematice disposita adjecta sua cuique speciei patria accedunt generum et specierum novarum diagnoses | publisher=Sumptibus Auctorum |page=156 |language=Latin | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/40220937 |access-date=November 21, 2025 }}</ref>

The buff-cheeked greenlet has two subspecies, the [[Nominate subspecies|nominate]] ''P. m. muscicapina'' ([[Philip Sclater|Sclater, PL]] & [[Osbert Salvin|Salvin]], 1873) and ''P. m. griseifrons'' ([[Emilie Snethlage|Snethlage, E]], 1907).<ref name=IOC15.1/>

[[File:Hylophilus muscicapinus Keulemans.jpg|thumb|left|180px|"''Hylophilus muscicapinus''" illustration by [[John Gerrard Keulemans|Keulemans]], 1881]]
==Description==

The buff-cheeked greenlet is {{convert|11.5|to|12|cm|in|sigfig=2|abbr=on}} long and weighs {{convert|9.5|to|13.5|g|oz|sigfig=2|abbr=on}}. The sexes have the same plumage. Adults of the nominate subspecies have a gray-brown forehead and a blue-gray crown and nape. They have a buffy [[supercilium]] and blackish gray mottled ear [[coverts]] on an otherwise buffy-brown face. Their upperparts are greenish that is lighter on the rump. Their wings' [[Primaries (birds)|primaries]] and [[Secondaries (birds)|secondaries]] are dark blackish gray with wide greenish edges on the outer webs. Their tail is dull greenish. Their chin and throat are off-white, their breast grayish buff, their belly grayish white, and their flanks yellowish. Subspecies ''P. m. griseifrons'' has a pure gray forehead and a deeper buffy rufous supercilium and face than the nominate with brighter green upperparts and a more buffy throat and breast. Both subspecies have a sepia, brown, or gray iris, a blackish [[maxilla]], a pinkish [[mandible]], and pale pinkish or light gray legs and feet.<ref name=BCGR-BOW>Brewer, D. (2020). Buff-cheeked Greenlet (''Pachysylvia muscicapina''), version 1.0. In Birds of the World (J. del Hoyo, A. Elliott, J. Sargatal, D. A. Christie, and E. de Juana, Editors). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.bucgre1.01 retrieved November 21, 2025</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==

The nominate subspecies of the buff-cheeked greenlet is the more northerly of the two. It is found in eastern Venezuela's [[Amazonas (Venezuelan state)|Amazonas]], [[Bolívar (state)|Bolívar]], and southern [[Delta Amacuro]] states east to the Atlantic through the Guianas and northern Brazil north of the Amazon.<ref name=BCGR-BOW/><ref name=Hilty>{{cite book | last =Hilty | first =Steven L. | title =Birds of Venezuela | publisher =Princeton University Press | edition =second | date =2003 | location =Princeton NJ | pages =680 | language =English }}</ref> Subspecies ''P. m. griseifrons'' is found in Brazil south of the Amazon between the [[Madeira River|Madeira]] and [[Tapajós River|Tapajós]] rivers and south to [[Mato Grosso]] and southern [[Goiás]] and into extreme northeastern Bolivia.<ref name=BCGR-BOW/> The species inhabits humid ''[[Forest#Tropical moist|terra firme]]'' and ''[[Várzea forest|várzea]]'' forest where it frequents the mid-story to canopy.<ref name=BCGR-BOW/> In Brazil it ranges in elevation from sea level to {{convert|600|m|ft|abbr=on}}.<ref name=vanPerlo>{{cite book | last =van Perlo | first = Ber| title =A Field Guide to the Birds of Brazil | publisher =Oxford University Press | date =2009 | location =New York | pages =338–339 | isbn =978-0-19-530155-7 }}</ref> It reaches {{convert|1100|m|ft|-2|abbr=on}} in Venezuela.<ref name=Hilty/>

==Behavior==
===Movement===

The buff-cheeked greenlet is believed to be a sedentary year-round resident.<ref name=BCGR-BOW/>

===Feeding===

The buff-cheeked greenlet's diet has not been fully examined but is known to include insects and spiders. It feeds actively, often hanging upside-down to glean prey from leaves. It usually forages in pairs or small family groups and frequently joins [[mixed-species feeding flock]]s.<ref name=BCGR-BOW/><ref name=Hilty/>

===Breeding===

The buff-cheeked greenlet is believed to breed between December and May in Venezuela. Nothing else is known about the species' breeding biology.<ref name=BCGR-BOW/><ref name=Hilty/>

{{birdsong|url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Pachysylvia-muscicapina |species=the buff-cheeked greenlet}}
===Vocalization===

The buff-cheeked greenlet's song is described as a "high, warbling ''weet-oh-weeréet''".<ref name=vanPerlo/>

==Status==

The [[IUCN]] has assessed the buff-cheeked greenlet as being of Least Concern. It has a large range; its population size is not known and is believed to be decreasing. No immediate threats have been identified.<ref name=IUCN/> It is considered "frequent to uncommon" in Brazil and "common" in Venezuela.<ref name=vanPerlo/><ref name=Hilty/>
